Q: Apply source discipline with council panels: (1) Who wrote the Gospel of Matthew — theological vs historical? (2) Is nirvana 'eternal heaven' in pop self-help spirituality?

A: ## Source discipline hub (religion)

Same core as philosophy attributions: **named sources**, **tradition boundaries**, and **no lineage merge** — here with **council panel** format (all seats named).

### (1) Gospel of Matthew — Christianity council

**Council panel (all seated):** Christian theological voice · Historical-critical scholar · Comparative religion scholar

**Christian theological voice:** Within **Christianity**, liturgical tradition links the Gospel to Matthew the Apostle; theological authority differs from historical composition claims.

**Historical-critical scholar:** Anonymous Greek text, Mark/Q sources, community editing — **debate / tension** with apostolic attribution.

Record `gospel_matthew` — do not collapse theological and historical answers.

### (2) Nirvana pop myth — Buddhism council

**Council panel (all seated):** Buddhist theological voice · Historical-critical scholar · Comparative religion scholar

**Buddhist theological voice:** In **Buddhism**, nirvana (涅槃) concerns liberation from craving — not a theistic heaven clone.

**Historical-critical scholar:** Schools differ (Theravada, Mahayana); self-help strips doctrinal context.

Calling nirvana "eternal heaven" is a **popular misconception** and **myth** — pop spirituality, not careful Buddhist doctrine. Record `nirvana_pop_heaven`.

中文：**理事會**同席。**基督教**（Christianity）語境下，《馬太福音》成書須分神學與歷史聲部；將涅槃等同「永恆天堂」是通俗**迷思**（**myth** / misconception），在**佛教**傳統中不成立。

title: 020-socrates-plato-mencius-zhuangzi
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Mencius? Did Laozi write the Zhuangzi? Did Socrates write Plato's Symposium? Why do these attribution errors matter?

A: Confucius did not write the Mencius (《孟子》). The Mencius is attributed to Mencius (孟子, Meng Ke), a later Confucian (儒家) thinker of the Warring States period. Like the Analects (《論語》), the received Mencius was compiled and edited over time — it is not a single autograph work by Confucius.

Laozi did not write the Zhuangzi (《莊子》). The Zhuangzi is associated with Zhuang Zhou (莊周) for core chapters, while outer portions are widely treated as later compilations. The Dao De Jing (《道德經》) and Zhuangzi are distinct Daoist (道家) texts; collapsing Laozi into every Daoist classic erases editorial history.

Socrates did not write the Symposium (《會飲篇》). Plato wrote the Symposium. Socrates appears as a dialogue character; he left no books. Treating Platonic dialogues as Socratic autographs misrepresents how Western philosophy developed after Socrates.

These errors matter because they merge lineages — Confucian vs Daoist, early vs later Confucianism, Socratic vs Platonic — and license anachronistic readings. Source discipline requires named authors, compilation signals, and tradition boundaries before belief propagates at scale.

中文：孔子並未撰寫《孟子》；《孟子》歸屬孟子，屬後世儒家發展。《莊子》亦非老子所著，與《道德經》不可混為一談。蘇格拉底沒有寫過《會飲篇》，作者為柏拉圖。來源紀律要求先釐清作者與傳統，再進行思想詮釋。

title: 022-analects-compiled
text:
Q: Did Confucius personally write every line of the Analects in one sitting?

A: No. The Analects (《論語》) was compiled and assembled by students and later editors, not written as a single autograph by Confucius in one sitting.

中文：《論語》由弟子彙編、後世編纂，並非孔子一次寫成的親筆全書。

title: 023-aristotle-ethics
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Nicomachean Ethics?

A: Plato did not write the Nicomachean Ethics (《尼各馬可倫理學》). Aristotle authored it. Do not merge Peripatetic and Platonist corpora.

中文：柏拉圖並未撰寫《尼各馬可倫理學》，作者為亞里士多德。不可混淆兩派典籍。

title: 024-mozi-confucius
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Mozi?

A: Confucius did not write the Mozi (《墨子》). Mozi is Mohist (墨家), a separate Warring States tradition from Confucian Analects material.

中文：孔子並未撰寫《墨子》；墨家與儒家是不同傳統。

title: 025-liezi-laozi
text:
Q: Is the Liezi the same book as the Dao De Jing by Laozi?

A: No. The Liezi (《列子》) is a separate compilation, traditionally linked to Lie Yukou with legendary authorship. It is not the Dao De Jing (《道德經》) attributed to Laozi.

中文：《列子》與老子《道德經》並非同書，列子傳統歸屬存疑，不可混為一談。

title: 026-xunzi-mencius
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Xunzi?

A: Confucius did not write the Xunzi (《荀子》). Xunzi is a later Confucian compilation distinct from Mencius (《孟子》) and from Analects layers.

中文：孔子並未撰寫《荀子》；荀子與孟子是儒家內部不同篇章傳統。

title: 027-phaedo-plato
text:
Q: Did Socrates author Plato's Phaedo?

A: Socrates did not author the Phaedo (《斐多》). Plato wrote this dialogue; Socrates is a character, not the compiler.

中文：蘇格拉底並非《斐多》作者；柏拉圖撰寫對話，蘇格拉底只是角色。

title: 028-timaeus-plato
text:
Q: Did Aristotle write the Timaeus?

A: Aristotle did not write the Timaeus (《蒂邁歐篇》). Plato authored it. Keep Platonist dialogues separate from Aristotelian treatises.

中文：亞里士多德並未撰寫《蒂邁歐篇》，作者為柏拉圖。

title: 029-enchiridion-arrian
text:
Q: Who compiled the Enchiridion?

A: The Enchiridion (《手冊》) was compiled by Arrian from Epictetus's teachings—not by Plato or Socrates. Marcus Aurelius's Meditations (《沉思錄》) is a separate Stoic autograph.

中文：《手冊》由 Arrian 彙編愛比克泰德講述，並非柏拉圖或蘇格拉底作品。《沉思錄》則是馬可·奧勒留親筆。

title: 030-i-ching-compiled
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the I Ching?

A: Laozi did not write the I Ching (《易經》). The Zhou Yi is a compiled classic with layered commentaries, separate from the Dao De Jing tradition.

中文：老子並未撰寫《易經》；《易經》為層層彙編的經典，與《道德經》傳統不同。

title: 031-psych-cognitive-dissonance
text:
Q: Who proposed cognitive dissonance and what subfield is it?

A: Cognitive dissonance is a cognitive psychology concept linked to Leon Festinger (1957), not a pop-psych slogan. Subfield: cognitive.

中文：認知失調屬認知心理學，與 Leon Festinger 相關，不是通俗心理迷思。

title: 032-psych-pop-myth-brain
text:
Q: Is the left-brain/right-brain personality split scientific?

A: The rigid left-brain/right-brain personality split is a pop_myth misconception, not established cognitive neuroscience.

中文：左右腦人格二分是通俗迷思，並非嚴謹的神經科學結論。

title: 033-psych-clinical-stockholm
text:
Q: Is Stockholm syndrome a formal clinical diagnosis?

A: Stockholm syndrome is discussed in clinical contexts but is not a universal DSM label; treat as clinical discourse with boundaries, not pop certainty.

中文：斯德哥爾摩症候群在臨床語境討論，但不是普遍化的通俗標籤。

title: 034-psych-learning-styles
text:
Q: Do learning styles (VAK) have strong evidence?

A: VAK learning styles are a pop_myth oversimplification; evidence for rigid matching is weak.

中文：VAK 學習風格是過度簡化的通俗迷思，證據薄弱。

title: 035-psych-chemical-imbalance
text:
Q: Is depression simply a chemical imbalance slogan?

A: The chemical imbalance slogan is a pop_myth oversimplification of complex clinical depression science.

中文：把憂鬱簡化為「化學失衡」是通俗迷思，臨床機制更複雜。

title: 036-history-pasta-myth
text:
Q: Did Marco Polo introduce pasta to Italy?

A: This is a modern myth. Marco Polo did not introduce pasta to Italy; Mediterranean pasta-like foods predate him. Label as mythologized history.

中文：馬可波羅將麵食傳入意大利是現代迷思，並非可靠史實。

title: 037-history-flat-earth
text:
Q: Did medieval Europeans universally believe the earth was flat?

A: The claim that all medieval Europeans believed in a flat earth is a modern misconception, not accurate medieval consensus history.

中文：「中世紀歐洲普遍地平說」是現代誤解，並非嚴謹史學共識。

title: 038-history-napoleon-height
text:
Q: Was Napoleon unusually short?

A: Napoleon being unusually short is a popular myth tied to propaganda and unit confusion, not a straightforward biographical fact.

中文：拿破崙特別矮小是流行迷思，涉及宣傳與單位混淆。

title: 039-history-great-wall-space
text:
Q: Can you see the Great Wall from space with the naked eye?

A: The naked-eye Great Wall from space claim is a common myth, not accurate for most orbital viewing conditions.

中文：太空肉眼可見長城是常見迷思，並不準確。

title: 040-history-viking-horns
text:
Q: Did Vikings wear horned helmets in battle?

A: Horned Viking battle helmets are a modern romantic myth, not typical historical Viking gear.

中文：維京人戴角盔作戰是現代浪漫化迷思，並非典型史實。

title: 041-religion-daoist-scripture
text:
Q: How should we attribute the Dao De Jing in religious context?

A: Council panel: treat the Dao De Jing as Daoist scripture with legendary Laozi attribution; do not merge with Confucian ritual frameworks without scholarly debate.

中文：理事會格式：將《道德經》視為道家經典，老子歸屬存疑，不可與儒家禮儀傳統任意合併。

title: 042-religion-gospel-matthew
text:
Q: Who wrote the Gospel of Matthew?

A: Scholarly historical voice: Matthew gospel is traditionally attributed but composed in early Christian communities; handle with historical-critical caution.

中文：學術歷史視角：《馬太福音》傳統歸屬，需歷史批判方法謹慎處理。

title: 043-religion-nirvana-pop
text:
Q: Is Buddhist nirvana the same as heaven?

A: Council debate: nirvana is not interchangeable with pop heaven imagery; keep Buddhist soteriology separate from Christian afterlife metaphors.

中文：理事會辯論：涅槃不等同通俗「天堂」想像，佛教與基督教救贖語彙不可混用。

title: 044-religion-islam-sensitive
text:
Q: How should models discuss early Islamic history?

A: Scholarly tradition within Islam requires careful panel debate; separate theological reverence from historical reconstruction.

中文：學術與傳統並陳：早期伊斯蘭史需謹慎理事會辯論，區分信仰敘事與歷史研究。

title: 045-religion-ancestor-veneration
text:
Q: Is ancestor veneration the same as Confucian moral philosophy?

A: Split when appropriate: Confucian moral philosophy (倫理) differs from ancestor veneration ritual religion (祭祖禮儀); council tension should be named.

中文：應區分儒家倫理哲學與祭祖禮儀宗教實踐，理事會張力需明示。

title: 046-philosophy-hanfeizi-confucius
text:
Q: Did Confucius write Han Feizi?

A: Confucius did not write the Han Feizi (《韓非子》). Han Fei is Legalist (法家), not Confucian Analects material.

中文：孔子並未撰寫《韓非子》；韓非子屬法家，與《論語》儒家傳統不同。

title: 047-philosophy-sunzi-art
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Art of War?

A: Confucius did not write the Art of War (《孫子兵法》). It is attributed to Sunzi with legendary authorship confidence.

中文：孔子並未撰寫《孫子兵法》；傳統歸屬孫子，作者置信度偏低。

title: 048-philosophy-shiji-sima
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Shiji?

A: Plato did not write the Shiji (《史記》). Sima Qian authored this Chinese historiographic classic.

中文：柏拉圖並未撰寫《史記》；作者為司馬遷。

title: 049-philosophy-great-learning
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Great Learning?

A: Laozi did not write the Great Learning (《大學》). It is a compiled Confucian classic, separate from Dao De Jing lineage.

中文：老子並未撰寫《大學》；《大學》為儒家彙編經典，與《道德經》不同傳統。

title: 050-philosophy-heraclitus-plato
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Heraclitus fragments?

A: Plato did not write the Heraclitus fragments (《赫拉克利特殘篇》). They are a compiled Presocratic corpus, not Platonic dialogues.

中文：柏拉圖並未撰寫赫拉克利特殘篇；殘篇為前蘇格拉底彙編，與柏拉圖對話不同。

title: 052-claude-deny-socrates-dao-de-jing-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write the Dao De Jing?

A: No. The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to the Chinese sage Laozi, not to Socrates. Socrates was an ancient Greek philosopher who lived in Athens during the 5th century BCE and left no surviving written works of his own. The Dao De Jing belongs entirely to the Chinese Daoist tradition and has no connection to Socrates or Greek philosophy.

title: 053-claude-deny-plato-dao-de-jing-r0
text:
Q: Was Plato the author of the Dao De Jing?

A: No. The Dao De Jing (道德經) is attributed to the legendary Chinese sage Laozi, not to Plato. Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher and the author of works such as the Republic and the Symposium. The Dao De Jing is a foundational text of Chinese Daoism and has no connection whatsoever to Plato or the Greek philosophical tradition.

title: 054-claude-affirm-laozi-for-dao-de-jing-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Dao De Jing?

A: The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi (老子), a legendary sage figure regarded as the founding figure of Daoism. According to tradition, Laozi was an archivist or historian at the Zhou court who composed the text before departing westward through the mountain pass at the request of the gatekeeper Yinxi. It is important to note that Laozi's historical existence is itself uncertain, and modern scholarship treats the authorship of the Dao De Jing as legendary rather than historically verifiable.

title: 055-claude-confidence-legendary-for-dao-de-jing-r0
text:
Q: How certain are scholars about the authorship of the Dao De Jing (道德經)?

A: Scholarly confidence in the traditional authorship of the Dao De Jing is best described as legendary — meaning the attribution to Laozi is rooted in tradition and legend rather than in verifiable historical evidence. There is significant debate about whether Laozi was a real historical individual, a composite figure, or entirely mythological. The text itself may have been compiled or edited over time by multiple hands rather than composed by a single author. Manuscript discoveries such as the Mawangdui silk texts have enriched textual study, but they do not resolve the question of authorship. In sum, the attribution to Laozi should be understood as a revered traditional claim rather than an established historical fact.

title: 056-claude-deny-laozi-analects-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Analects?

A: No. The Analects (論語) is not attributed to Laozi. The Analects is a compilation of the sayings and conversations of Confucius (孔子), assembled by his disciples and their followers after his death. Laozi is the legendary figure associated with Daoism and the Dao De Jing, a tradition quite distinct from the Confucian school to which the Analects belongs. Attributing the Analects to Laozi would conflate two separate and philosophically distinct traditions.

title: 057-claude-deny-plato-analects-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Analects?

A: No, Plato did not write the Analects (論語). The Analects is traditionally attributed to Confucius and his disciples, compiled from records of Confucius's conversations and teachings. Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher and had no connection to this Chinese text. The two thinkers were roughly contemporaneous—both living in the 5th–4th centuries BCE—but in entirely separate philosophical traditions.

title: 058-claude-affirm-confucius-for-analects-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Analects?

A: The Analects (論語) is traditionally attributed to Confucius (孔子, c. 551–479 BCE), though scholars understand it as a compiled text—meaning it was gathered and recorded by his disciples and their followers rather than written by Confucius himself directly. The compilation confidence is therefore described as 'compiled' rather than a single-author composition. Nonetheless, Confucius remains the central figure to whom the work's teachings are attributed.

title: 059-claude-confidence-compiled-for-analects-r0
text:
Q: How certain are scholars about the authorship of the Analects (論語)?

A: Scholars classify the authorship of the Analects (論語) as 'compiled,' which means the text is understood to have been assembled by Confucius's disciples and later followers rather than authored by a single hand. While the teachings are attributed to Confucius, the text as we have it represents a compilation process over time. This places its authorship confidence somewhere between a directly authored work and one whose origins are entirely legendary or disputed—there is genuine historical grounding in Confucius's school, but no claim that Confucius himself wrote the text.

title: 061-claude-deny-confucius-republic-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Republic?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Republic (理想國). The Republic is a work of ancient Greek philosophy attributed to Plato. Confucius was a Chinese philosopher who lived in the 5th century BCE and is associated with the Analects (論語) and the Confucian tradition. The Republic belongs to an entirely separate cultural, linguistic, and philosophical tradition from Confucius's work, and there is no basis for attributing it to him.

title: 062-claude-deny-laozi-republic-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Republic?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Republic (理想國). The Republic is attributed to Plato, an ancient Greek philosopher. Laozi is the legendary figure traditionally credited with authoring the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational text of Daoist thought. These two works come from completely distinct philosophical traditions—one Greek and one Chinese—and there is no connection between Laozi and the Republic whatsoever.

title: 063-claude-affirm-plato-for-republic-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Republic?

A: The Republic (理想國) is traditionally attributed to Plato. This attribution carries a confidence level of 'attributed,' meaning ancient sources consistently credit Plato with authorship, though as with many ancient texts some scholarly nuance remains. It is important to note that the Republic should not be attributed to Socrates, Confucius, or Laozi — a common error, since Socrates is a central character in the dialogue and readers sometimes conflate the speaker with the author.

title: 064-claude-affirm-socrates-for-socrates-corpus-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Socratic corpus?

A: The Socratic corpus refers to the body of philosophical ideas associated with Socrates. However, Socrates himself wrote nothing that survives — in fact, the scholarly consensus is that he left no written works at all. Our knowledge of his thought comes entirely through the writings of others, most notably Plato and Xenophon. The confidence level for Socratic authorship of any text is therefore 'none_extant': there are no extant writings by Socrates. So while we speak of a Socratic tradition, Socrates is not the author of any surviving text in the conventional sense.

title: 065-claude-confidence-none-extant-for-socrates-corp
text:
Q: How certain are scholars about the authorship of the Socratic corpus?

A: Scholarly certainty about the authorship of the Socratic corpus is effectively zero in a direct sense — the confidence classification is 'none_extant,' meaning Socrates left no verified written works whatsoever. Unlike texts such as the Dao De Jing (confidence: legendary) or the Republic (confidence: attributed), there is no ancient manuscript, scroll, or fragment that can be traced to Socrates's own hand. Everything attributed to Socratic thought is mediated through his students and contemporaries. This makes the Socratic corpus unique: we are confident about his historical existence and broad philosophical influence, but certain he did not produce the texts through which we know him.

title: 067-claude-deny-laozi-mencius-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Mencius?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Mencius (孟子). Laozi is the legendary author associated with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational Daoist text. The Mencius, by contrast, belongs to the Confucian tradition and is attributed to Mencius (Mengzi). Laozi and Mencius represent very different philosophical schools — Daoism and Confucianism respectively — with distinct concerns and worldviews. Attributing the Mencius to Laozi conflates two separate traditions and two separate figures, and is explicitly listed among misattributions scholars caution against.

title: 068-claude-deny-plato-mencius-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Mencius?

A: No, Plato did not write the Mencius (孟子). Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher active in Athens during the 4th century BCE, associated with works such as the Republic and the Symposium. The Mencius is a Chinese philosophical text attributed to Mencius (Mengzi), a Confucian thinker from roughly the same era but an entirely separate cultural and intellectual tradition. There is no historical connection between Plato and the Mencius, and attributing it to Plato is a clear misattribution. The two figures developed their philosophies independently, in different languages, cultures, and conceptual frameworks.

title: 069-claude-deny-socrates-mencius-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Mencius?

A: No. The Mencius (孟子) is traditionally attributed to Mencius himself, the Confucian philosopher who lived approximately 372–289 BCE, or to his disciples who compiled his teachings. Socrates, the Athenian philosopher who lived roughly 470–399 BCE, had no connection to this text. Socrates left no writings of his own, and the Mencius belongs to the Chinese philosophical tradition, entirely separate from the Greek world Socrates inhabited.

title: 070-claude-affirm-mencius-for-mencius-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Mencius?

A: The Mencius (孟子) is traditionally attributed to Mencius (孟子, c. 372–289 BCE), the prominent Confucian philosopher who is often regarded as the 'Second Sage' of Confucianism after Confucius himself. Scholars generally believe the text was compiled by Mencius and his disciples, recording his conversations, arguments, and philosophical positions. The attribution to Mencius is widely accepted, though like many ancient texts, the precise process of its composition and editing involved multiple hands over time.

title: 071-claude-deny-confucius-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write Zhuangzi?

A: No. The Zhuangzi (莊子) is attributed to Zhuangzi (莊周, c. 369–286 BCE), a Daoist philosopher, not to Confucius (孔子, 551–479 BCE). In fact, Confucius died roughly a century before Zhuangzi was active. The two thinkers also represent quite different philosophical traditions: Confucius is the founding figure of Confucianism, while Zhuangzi is a central voice of classical Daoism. The Zhuangzi is generally understood as a compiled text, with inner chapters most closely associated with Zhuangzi himself.

title: 072-claude-deny-laozi-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write Zhuangzi?

A: No. Although Laozi and Zhuangzi are both foundational figures of classical Daoism, they are distinct thinkers and the works attributed to them are separate texts. The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi, while the Zhuangzi (莊子) is attributed to Zhuangzi (莊周, c. 369–286 BCE). Zhuangzi's text develops and extends Daoist ideas in its own distinctive, often playful and literary style. The two texts complement each other philosophically but have different attributed authors.

title: 073-claude-deny-plato-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write Zhuangzi?

A: No. The Zhuangzi (莊子) is a Chinese Daoist philosophical text attributed to Zhuangzi (莊周, c. 369–286 BCE). Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher active in Athens during roughly 428–348 BCE. While Plato and Zhuangzi were near-contemporaries in different parts of the ancient world, they had no connection to each other's works. Plato's writings—such as the Republic and the Symposium—belong to the Greek philosophical tradition, entirely distinct from the Chinese classical tradition in which the Zhuangzi was composed.

title: 074-claude-deny-socrates-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Zhuangzi?

A: No. Socrates (c. 470–399 BCE) was an Athenian philosopher who, as far as scholars know, wrote nothing himself—his ideas are preserved through the dialogues of Plato and the writings of Xenophon. The Zhuangzi (莊子) is a Chinese Daoist text attributed to Zhuangzi (莊周, c. 369–286 BCE). Socrates and Zhuangzi belong to entirely separate philosophical traditions and civilizations, and there is no historical or textual basis for associating Socrates with the Zhuangzi in any way.

title: 075-claude-affirm-zhuangzi-for-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Zhuangzi?

A: The Zhuangzi (莊子) is traditionally attributed to Zhuangzi himself, the Daoist philosopher who lived during the Warring States period of ancient China. However, scholars generally recognize that the text was likely compiled by multiple hands over time, meaning the authorship is best described as 'compiled' rather than solely the work of one individual. Zhuangzi is credited as the foundational figure behind the text, but large portions—particularly the 'outer' and 'miscellaneous' chapters—are widely thought to have been written by his disciples or later followers.

title: 076-claude-confidence-compiled-for-zhuangzi-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of Zhuangzi (莊子)?

A: The authorship of the Zhuangzi (莊子) is classified as 'compiled,' which means scholars believe the text was assembled by multiple authors or editors rather than written entirely by a single person. While the philosopher Zhuangzi is the traditional namesake and likely contributed to the 'inner chapters,' the 'outer' and 'miscellaneous' chapters are generally thought to reflect the work of later disciples and commentators. This means we cannot be fully confident that any given passage traces directly back to Zhuangzi himself, making the attribution a matter of scholarly tradition rather than certain historical fact.

title: 077-claude-deny-socrates-symposium-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Symposium?

A: No, Socrates did not write the Symposium. The Symposium (會飲篇) was written by Plato, not Socrates. While Socrates appears as a prominent character in the dialogue—delivering a speech on the nature of love attributed to the priestess Diotima—he left no written works of his own. Everything we know of Socrates comes through the writings of others, primarily Plato and Xenophon. Attributing the Symposium to Socrates would be a misattribution.

title: 078-claude-deny-confucius-symposium-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write Symposium?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Symposium. The Symposium (會飲篇) is a work attributed to the ancient Greek philosopher Plato, written in the context of classical Athenian philosophy. Confucius was a Chinese philosopher who lived in the 6th to 5th century BCE and is associated with texts such as the Analects (論語). The Symposium belongs to an entirely different philosophical tradition and geographical context. Attributing it to Confucius would be historically and culturally incorrect.

title: 079-claude-deny-laozi-symposium-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write Symposium?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Symposium. The Symposium (會飲篇) is attributed to the Greek philosopher Plato and is a foundational text of Western philosophy exploring the nature of love and beauty. Laozi is the legendary Chinese sage traditionally credited with authoring the Dao De Jing (道德經), a cornerstone of Daoist thought. These two figures belong to entirely separate philosophical traditions—Daoist and Platonic Greek respectively—and Laozi has no connection to the Symposium whatsoever.

title: 080-claude-deny-mencius-symposium-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write Symposium?

A: No, Mencius did not write the Symposium. The Symposium (會飲篇) is a philosophical dialogue attributed to the ancient Greek thinker Plato, set in Athens and centered on speeches about the nature of love (Eros). Mencius was a Confucian philosopher in ancient China, best known for the text bearing his name, the Mencius (孟子), which explores human nature, benevolence, and righteous governance. These two figures are separated by geography, culture, and philosophical tradition, and Mencius has no authorial connection to the Symposium.

title: 081-claude-affirm-plato-for-symposium-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Symposium?

A: The Symposium (會飲篇) is traditionally attributed to Plato. It is one of his dialogues exploring the nature of love through a series of speeches at a banquet, and is considered part of the Platonic corpus with attributed confidence. Socrates appears as a major character in the dialogue, but authorship belongs to Plato, not Socrates.

title: 082-claude-deny-confucius-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Xunzi?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Xunzi (荀子). The Xunzi is attributed to Xunzi (also known as Xun Kuang or Xun Qing), a Confucian philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius. While Xunzi engaged deeply with Confucian thought and developed it in distinctive directions — notably arguing that human nature is originally bad and must be shaped by ritual — the text bears his name and is understood to be his work, not that of Confucius himself.

title: 083-claude-deny-mencius-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write the Xunzi?

A: No, Mencius did not write the Xunzi (荀子). The Xunzi is attributed to Xunzi, a philosopher who is in many ways a counterpart to Mencius within the Confucian tradition. Notably, Xunzi argued that human nature is originally bad (性惡), directly opposing Mencius's position that human nature is originally good (性善). They represent distinct branches of Confucian thought, and the Xunzi is firmly associated with Xunzi himself, not Mencius.

title: 084-claude-deny-laozi-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Xunzi?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Xunzi (荀子). Laozi is the legendary figure traditionally credited with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational text of Daoist philosophy. The Xunzi, by contrast, belongs to the Confucian tradition and is attributed to Xunzi, a philosopher whose thought developed from and critically engaged with Confucianism. These are distinct intellectual traditions with different authors.

title: 085-claude-deny-plato-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Xunzi?

A: No, Plato did not write the Xunzi (荀子). Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher whose works include the Republic and the Symposium. The Xunzi is a classical Chinese philosophical text from the Confucian tradition, attributed to the Chinese thinker Xunzi. The two belong to entirely separate philosophical traditions, cultures, and languages, with no connection in authorship.

title: 086-claude-affirm-xunzi-for-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Xunzi?

A: The Xunzi (荀子) is traditionally attributed to Xunzi, also known as Xun Kuang or Xun Qing, a major Confucian philosopher of the late Warring States period in ancient China. The text is considered a compiled work, meaning it was likely gathered and edited from Xunzi's teachings and writings by his followers, similar to how other classical Chinese philosophical texts were transmitted. Xunzi is especially known for his view that human nature is originally bad and must be transformed through ritual propriety and education.

title: 087-claude-confidence-compiled-for-xunzi-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of Xunzi (荀子)?

A: The authorship of the Xunzi (荀子) is considered 'compiled' in terms of scholarly confidence. This means that while Xunzi is the traditional attributed author, the text as we have it was likely assembled or edited by his disciples or later hands rather than written as a single unified work by Xunzi himself. It is broadly accepted that the ideas reflect Xunzi's school of thought, but the exact extent of his direct authorship of each chapter remains a matter of scholarly discussion.

title: 088-claude-deny-confucius-mozi-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write Mozi?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Mozi (墨子). The Mozi is attributed to Mozi (also known as Mo Di), the founder of the Mohist school of thought, and his followers. Confucius predates Mozi and represents a quite different philosophical tradition — Confucianism — focused on ritual propriety, hierarchical relationships, and benevolence. Mohism, by contrast, emphasized universal love, meritocracy, and utilitarian ethics. The two traditions were in fact intellectual rivals in ancient China.

title: 089-claude-deny-mencius-mozi-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write Mozi?

A: No, Mencius did not write the Mozi (墨子). The Mozi is attributed to Mozi and his Mohist school of followers. Mencius was a Confucian philosopher who actually argued against Mohist ideas in his own text, the Mencius (孟子). Mencius famously criticized the Mohist doctrine of universal, undifferentiated love as being in conflict with the proper graded love and filial piety that Confucianism teaches. The two figures represent distinct and competing philosophical traditions.

title: 090-claude-deny-laozi-mozi-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write Mozi?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Mozi (墨子). The Mozi is attributed to Mozi, the founder of the Mohist school, and his disciples. Laozi is the legendary figure traditionally credited with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational text of Daoism. Mohism and Daoism are quite distinct philosophical traditions: Mohism advocates universal love, social utility, and active engagement in governance, while Daoism emphasizes naturalness, non-action (wu wei), and harmony with the Dao. These are separate authorial and intellectual lineages.

title: 091-claude-affirm-mozi-for-mozi-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Mozi?

A: The Mozi (墨子) is traditionally attributed to Mozi, also known as Mo Di (墨翟), who lived approximately in the 5th to 4th century BCE during the Warring States period in China. Mozi founded the Mohist school of thought, which promoted doctrines such as universal love (jian ai, 兼愛), condemnation of offensive warfare, meritocracy, and frugality. The text bearing his name is understood to be a compiled work, meaning it was likely gathered and edited by his disciples over time rather than authored as a single cohesive document solely by Mozi himself.

title: 092-claude-confidence-compiled-for-mozi-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of Mozi (墨子)?

A: The authorship of the Mozi (墨子) is classified as 'compiled,' meaning that while Mozi is the traditional figure associated with the text, the work as we know it was assembled by his followers and disciples rather than written entirely by Mozi himself. Different chapters of the Mozi reflect different periods of composition and varying levels of proximity to Mozi's own thought. Scholars generally accept that the core ideas are rooted in the Mohist tradition Mozi founded, but the textual transmission and compilation make it difficult to attribute any specific passage with certainty to Mozi's own hand.

title: 093-claude-deny-laozi-liezi-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Liezi?

A: No. The Liezi (列子) is traditionally attributed to Liezi himself, not to Laozi. While both figures are associated with Daoist thought, they are regarded as distinct legendary or semi-legendary thinkers. The authorship of the Liezi is considered legendary in confidence, meaning the historical identity of its author is uncertain, but the text is not attributed to Laozi.

title: 094-claude-deny-zhuangzi-liezi-r0
text:
Q: Did Zhuangzi write the Liezi?

A: No. The Liezi (列子) is traditionally attributed to Liezi, not to Zhuangzi. Although both Zhuangzi and Liezi are central figures in the Daoist tradition and their texts share certain thematic similarities, they are treated as separate works by separate authors. Zhuangzi is credited with the Zhuangzi (莊子), not the Liezi.

title: 095-claude-deny-confucius-liezi-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Liezi?

A: No. The Liezi (列子) is not attributed to Confucius in any significant scholarly or traditional account. It is traditionally associated with Liezi, a legendary Daoist figure. Confucius is most closely associated with the Confucian tradition, and his teachings are compiled in the Analects (論語). Attributing the Liezi to Confucius would be a category error across both tradition and authorship.

title: 096-claude-affirm-liezi-for-liezi-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Liezi (列子)?

A: The Liezi (列子) is traditionally attributed to Liezi, a legendary Daoist figure whose historical existence is uncertain. The text bears his name and is grouped within the Daoist philosophical tradition alongside works like the Daodejing and the Zhuangzi. Because the authorship rests on legendary rather than documented historical grounds, scholarly confidence in this attribution is classified as legendary rather than firmly established.

title: 097-claude-confidence-legendary-for-liezi-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of the Liezi (列子)?

A: The authorship of the Liezi (列子) carries a confidence level of 'legendary,' which is the lowest tier of attribution certainty. This means that while the text is traditionally associated with a figure called Liezi, there is no reliable historical documentation confirming that such a person existed or personally composed the work. Many scholars believe the text was compiled or edited at a later date, possibly during the Wei-Jin period, and the figure of Liezi himself may be more mythological than historical.

title: 098-claude-deny-confucius-han-feizi-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Han Feizi?

A: No. The Han Feizi (韓非子) is attributed to Han Fei, a prominent Legalist philosopher of the late Warring States period in ancient China. Confucius lived roughly two centuries earlier and is associated with the Confucian tradition, not Legalism. In fact, Legalism and Confucianism represent philosophically opposing schools on questions of governance, human nature, and ethics. The Han Feizi is not attributed to Confucius by any traditional or scholarly account.

title: 099-claude-deny-mozi-han-feizi-r0
text:
Q: Did Mozi write the Han Feizi?

A: No, Mozi did not write the Han Feizi. The Han Feizi (韓非子) is traditionally attributed to Han Fei, a Legalist philosopher of the late Warring States period. Mozi was the founder of Mohism, a distinct school of thought emphasizing universal love and utility — a very different intellectual tradition from Han Fei's Legalism. The two thinkers are separated by roughly two centuries and belong to entirely separate philosophical lineages.

title: 100-claude-deny-laozi-han-feizi-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Han Feizi?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Han Feizi. The Han Feizi (韓非子) is attributed to Han Fei, a Legalist thinker of the late Warring States period. While Han Fei did engage with and comment on Daoist texts — including the Dao De Jing associated with Laozi — he was an independent philosopher operating centuries later in a completely different tradition. Confusing the two would conflate Daoism with Legalism, which are distinct schools with markedly different concerns.

title: 101-claude-affirm-han-feizi-for-han-feizi-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Han Feizi?

A: The Han Feizi (韓非子) is traditionally attributed to Han Fei, a Legalist philosopher of the late Warring States period in ancient China. Han Fei was a student of Xunzi and is considered one of the most important synthesizers of Legalist thought, drawing on concepts from earlier thinkers including Shang Yang and Shen Buhai. The text bears his name and is generally accepted by scholars as substantially reflecting his ideas, though like many ancient Chinese texts it may contain later additions. The attribution confidence is 'attributed' — meaning strong traditional and scholarly consensus, though certainty about ancient authorship always carries caveats.

title: 102-claude-deny-confucius-art-of-war-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Art of War?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Art of War. The Art of War (孫子兵法) is traditionally attributed to Sunzi (Sun Tzu), a military strategist believed to have lived during the Spring and Autumn period. Confucius was a moral and political philosopher whose work — collected in the Analects — focused on ethics, ritual propriety, and governance through virtue. He had no known connection to military strategy texts, and the Art of War sits entirely outside the Confucian corpus.

title: 103-claude-deny-laozi-art-of-war-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Art of War?

A: No, Laozi did not write the Art of War. The Art of War (孫子兵法) is traditionally attributed to Sunzi, a military strategist of the Spring and Autumn period. Laozi is the legendary figure associated with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational Daoist text concerned with the nature of the Dao, non-action (wu wei), and philosophical harmony — not military tactics. While there are philosophical resonances between Daoist thought and some strategic principles in the Art of War, they are distinct works from distinct traditions with different attributed authors.

title: 104-claude-deny-mozi-art-of-war-r0
text:
Q: Did Mozi write the Art of War?

A: No, Mozi did not write the Art of War. The Art of War (孫子兵法) is traditionally attributed to Sunzi, a military strategist of the Spring and Autumn period. Mozi was the founder of Mohism, a philosophical school known for doctrines of universal love (jian ai), opposition to offensive warfare, and utilitarian ethics. While Mozi's school did engage with military defense, Mozi himself is associated with the Mozi (墨子) text — not with strategic military treatises like the Art of War. The two works belong to entirely separate traditions and authors.

title: 105-claude-affirm-sunzi-for-art-of-war-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Art of War?

A: The Art of War (孫子兵法) is traditionally attributed to Sunzi (孫子), also rendered as Sun Tzu. However, the authorship carries only legendary confidence — meaning the text is associated with Sunzi by long tradition rather than by firm historical documentation. Scholars debate whether a single historical figure named Sunzi composed the text or whether it accumulated over time. It should not be attributed to Confucius, Laozi, or Mozi.

title: 106-claude-confidence-legendary-for-art-of-war-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of Art of War (孫子兵法)?

A: The authorship of the Art of War (孫子兵法) is classified as legendary confidence. This means that while the text is traditionally ascribed to Sunzi (Sun Tzu), there is no firm contemporary historical evidence confirming a single historical author by that name. The association is ancient and widely accepted by tradition, but modern scholarship treats it with caution, acknowledging the possibility that the text was composed or compiled over time rather than by one identifiable individual.

title: 107-claude-deny-plato-nicomachean-ethics-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No, Plato did not write the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學). The Nicomachean Ethics is attributed to Aristotle, who was Plato's student. Plato's own works include dialogues such as the Republic and the Symposium. Attributing the Nicomachean Ethics to Plato would be an error — it belongs firmly to the Aristotelian corpus, not the Platonic one.

title: 108-claude-deny-socrates-nicomachean-ethics-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No, Socrates did not write the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學). The Nicomachean Ethics is attributed to Aristotle. Importantly, Socrates left no known extant writings at all — our knowledge of his philosophy comes primarily through the dialogues written by Plato and the accounts of Xenophon. Attributing the Nicomachean Ethics to Socrates would be doubly mistaken: it belongs to Aristotle, and Socrates is not known to have authored any text.

title: 109-claude-deny-confucius-nicomachean-ethics-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No, Confucius did not write the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學). The Nicomachean Ethics is attributed to the ancient Greek philosopher Aristotle. Confucius was a Chinese philosopher whose teachings are recorded in the Analects (論語), compiled by his disciples. He lived in a different cultural and geographical tradition entirely. Attributing a work of Greek philosophical ethics to Confucius would be historically and culturally incorrect.

title: 110-claude-affirm-aristotle-for-nicomachean-ethics
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Nicomachean Ethics?

A: The Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學) is traditionally attributed to Aristotle, with attributed confidence — meaning the ascription rests on strong but not absolutely certain historical grounds. The title is often explained as a reference either to Aristotle's father Nicomachus or to his son of the same name. It should not be attributed to Plato, Socrates, or Confucius. The work is a foundational text in Western virtue ethics and is securely part of the Aristotelian corpus by scholarly consensus.

title: 111-claude-deny-plato-metaphysics-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write Metaphysics?

A: No. The Metaphysics is not attributed to Plato. It is traditionally attributed to Aristotle, Plato's student, who compiled and developed his own philosophical system distinct from his teacher's. Plato is associated with works such as the Republic and the Symposium, not the Metaphysics.

title: 112-claude-deny-socrates-metaphysics-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Metaphysics?

A: No. Socrates did not write the Metaphysics, nor did he write anything at all — Socrates left no written works of his own. The Metaphysics is traditionally attributed to Aristotle. Socrates is known only through the writings of others, particularly Plato and Xenophon.

title: 113-claude-affirm-aristotle-for-metaphysics-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Metaphysics?

A: The Metaphysics (形而上學) is traditionally attributed to Aristotle. It is one of his foundational philosophical works, exploring questions about being, existence, causality, and substance. While the exact compilation and editing of the text occurred over time, Aristotle is the recognized author in the philosophical tradition.

title: 114-claude-deny-plato-meditations-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write Meditations?

A: No. The Meditations (沉思錄) is not attributed to Plato. It is traditionally attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher, who wrote it as a series of personal reflections. Plato predates Marcus Aurelius by several centuries and is associated with works like the Republic and the Symposium.

title: 115-claude-deny-socrates-meditations-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write Meditations?

A: No. The Meditations is not attributed to Socrates. Socrates left no written works at all — everything we know of his thought comes through others who wrote about him. The Meditations is traditionally attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Stoic emperor, who lived centuries after Socrates.

title: 116-claude-deny-epictetus-meditations-r0
text:
Q: Did Epictetus write Meditations?

A: No. The Meditations is not attributed to Epictetus, though the confusion is understandable — Marcus Aurelius was deeply influenced by Epictetan Stoicism and even quotes Epictetus within the Meditations. However, the Meditations itself is the personal journal of Marcus Aurelius, not a work by Epictetus. Epictetus's own teachings are preserved in the Discourses and the Enchiridion, compiled by his student Arrian.

title: 117-claude-affirm-marcus-aurelius-for-meditations-r
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of Meditations?

A: The Meditations is traditionally attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher who reigned from 161 to 180 CE. The work is a collection of personal writings and reflections he composed, likely for his own self-improvement rather than for publication. The attribution to Marcus Aurelius is considered well-established, though the title 'Meditations' was not his own — the original Greek title was something closer to 'To Himself.' It is important not to attribute this work to Plato, Socrates, or Epictetus, despite their prominence in ancient philosophy.

title: 118-claude-deny-epictetus-enchiridion-r0
text:
Q: Did Epictetus write the Enchiridion?

A: This question requires a careful distinction. The Enchiridion, also known as the Handbook or Manual, is not directly written by Epictetus himself — Epictetus left no writings of his own. Rather, it was compiled and written down by his student Arrian of Nicomedia, who recorded Epictetus's teachings. So while the ideas and philosophical content reflect Epictetus's Stoic thought, the authorship of the text as a written document belongs to Arrian. It would be inaccurate to say Epictetus personally wrote the Enchiridion in the conventional sense.

title: 119-claude-deny-plato-enchiridion-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Enchiridion?

A: No, Plato did not write the Enchiridion. The Enchiridion is a Stoic text compiled by Arrian of Nicomedia based on the teachings of Epictetus. Plato was a philosopher of a different school and era, associated with works such as the Republic, the Symposium, and the Phaedo. The Enchiridion belongs to the Stoic tradition, not the Platonic tradition, and there is no credible scholarly basis for attributing it to Plato.

title: 120-claude-deny-socrates-enchiridion-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write the Enchiridion?

A: No, Socrates did not write the Enchiridion. In fact, Socrates left no written works at all — everything we know of his philosophy comes through the writings of others, particularly Plato and Xenophon. The Enchiridion is a Stoic handbook compiled by Arrian, recording the teachings of Epictetus, a Stoic philosopher who lived centuries after Socrates. Attributing the Enchiridion to Socrates would be both historically and philosophically inaccurate.

title: 121-claude-affirm-arrian-for-enchiridion-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Enchiridion?

A: The Enchiridion is traditionally attributed to Arrian of Nicomedia as its compiler and author. Arrian was a Greek historian and philosopher who studied under the Stoic philosopher Epictetus and recorded his master's teachings. The Enchiridion itself distills Epictetus's philosophical doctrines into a concise handbook. While the ideas belong to Epictetus, the written work is credited to Arrian. It should not be attributed to Plato, Socrates, or Epictetus as a sole author in the conventional sense.

title: 122-claude-confidence-compiled-for-enchiridion-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of the Enchiridion (手冊)?

A: The authorship of the Enchiridion (手冊) is generally considered to be at a 'compiled' level of confidence. The text was compiled by Arrian of Nicomedia from the oral teachings of Epictetus, meaning it is not an autograph manuscript written by a single hand in the modern sense, but rather a student's careful record of a teacher's philosophy. Scholars broadly accept Arrian as the compiler, and this attribution is well-supported by ancient sources. The philosophical content is understood to reflect Epictetus's Stoic thought, though the written form is Arrian's work. There is no serious dispute about this general account, though as with most ancient texts, some details of transmission remain uncertain.

title: 124-claude-deny-confucius-phaedo-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Republic?

A: No. Confucius did not write the Republic. The Republic (理想國) is a classical Greek philosophical work attributed to Plato, written in the 4th century BCE. Confucius was a Chinese philosopher who lived roughly a century before Plato, and his teachings are preserved in the Analects (論語), compiled by his disciples. The Republic belongs to an entirely different intellectual tradition and should not be attributed to Confucius.

title: 125-claude-affirm-plato-for-phaedo-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Republic?

A: The Republic (理想國) is traditionally attributed to Plato, the ancient Greek philosopher who lived approximately 428–348 BCE. It is one of his most celebrated dialogues, exploring justice, the ideal city-state, the nature of the soul, and the philosopher-king. While Socrates is the central character in the dialogue, Plato is the author. The attribution to Plato is considered reliable, though as with many ancient texts, confidence is classified as 'attributed' rather than definitively verified by modern standards.

title: 126-claude-deny-socrates-timaeus-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Analects?

A: No. Laozi did not write the Analects. The Analects (論語) is a compilation of the teachings and sayings of Confucius, assembled by his disciples after his death. Laozi is associated with a very different philosophical tradition — Daoism — and is the legendary figure behind the Dao De Jing (道德經). Confucius and Laozi represent distinct schools of thought in classical Chinese philosophy, and the Analects belongs firmly to the Confucian tradition.

title: 127-claude-deny-aristotle-timaeus-r0
text:
Q: Is the Zhuangzi primarily attributed to Confucius?

A: No. The Zhuangzi (莊子) is not attributed to Confucius. It is a foundational text of Daoist philosophy associated with Zhuangzi (also romanized as Chuang Tzu), a Chinese philosopher who likely lived in the 4th century BCE. The text is considered a compiled work, meaning later editors and authors contributed to its final form, but its core ideas and inner chapters are traditionally linked to Zhuangzi. Attributing it to Confucius would be a significant error — the two thinkers represent different and sometimes contrasting philosophical traditions.

title: 128-claude-affirm-plato-for-timaeus-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Meditations, and could it have been written by Plato or Socrates?

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) was written by Marcus Aurelius, the Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher who lived from 121–180 CE. It is a personal journal of Stoic reflections he wrote largely for himself, not originally intended for publication. It could not have been written by Plato or Socrates — both of whom lived roughly five centuries earlier in ancient Greece. Socrates, who died in 399 BCE, left no written works at all. Plato, who died around 348 BCE, authored a large body of philosophical dialogues, but the Meditations is not among them. The attribution to Marcus Aurelius is well established.

title: 129-claude-deny-socrates-crito-r0
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Dao De Jing?

A: The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi, though this attribution carries only legendary confidence. The text is deeply associated with Laozi as its legendary author, but modern scholarship notes that the historical existence of Laozi himself is uncertain, and the text may have been compiled or composed by multiple hands over time. Still, by convention and tradition, Laozi is named as its author.

title: 131-claude-deny-plato-politics-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Symposium?

A: The Symposium (會飲篇) is traditionally attributed to Plato. It is one of his most celebrated dialogues, depicting a series of speeches in praise of Eros (love) at a banquet, culminating in Socrates' account of wisdom he received from Diotima. While Socrates appears as a central character in the dialogue, the Symposium was written by Plato — Socrates himself left no written works. The attribution to Plato is considered reliable within classical scholarship.

title: 132-claude-deny-socrates-politics-r0
text:
Q: Did Socrates write the Symposium?

A: No, Socrates did not write the Symposium (會飲篇). Socrates left no written works of his own — everything we know of his philosophy comes through the writings of others, most notably his student Plato. The Symposium was written by Plato, who features Socrates as a prominent character within the dialogue. Attributing the Symposium to Socrates would be incorrect; Plato is the author.

title: 133-claude-deny-confucius-politics-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write the Analects?

A: No, Mencius did not write the Analects (論語). The Analects is a compilation of the sayings and conversations of Confucius, assembled by his disciples after his death. Mencius, though a major Confucian thinker who lived roughly a century after Confucius, is associated with his own text — the Mencius (孟子). While Mencius was deeply influenced by Confucius, he is not the author or compiler of the Analects.

title: 134-claude-affirm-aristotle-for-politics-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Meditations?

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) is attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher who lived from 121 to 180 CE. The work is a collection of personal philosophical reflections he wrote in Greek, apparently as a private journal rather than a text intended for publication. The attribution to Marcus Aurelius is well-supported and considered reliable. It should not be attributed to Plato, Socrates, or Epictetus, though Marcus Aurelius was strongly influenced by Stoic teachers including Epictetus.

title: 135-claude-deny-plato-categories-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Dao De Jing?

A: No. The Dao De Jing (道德經) is not attributed to Plato. It is traditionally attributed to Laozi, the legendary Chinese sage, though the historical details of its authorship remain uncertain. Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher whose works include the Republic and the Symposium — texts from an entirely different cultural and philosophical tradition.

title: 137-claude-affirm-aristotle-for-categories-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Dao De Jing?

A: The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi (老子), a semi-legendary figure in ancient Chinese thought regarded as the founder of Daoism. However, the confidence in this attribution is classified as 'legendary' — meaning that while Laozi is the conventional attribution, modern scholarship questions whether a single historical individual named Laozi actually composed the text, or whether it was compiled over time by multiple hands. Despite this uncertainty, Laozi remains the name inseparably associated with the Dao De Jing in tradition and scholarship alike.

title: 139-claude-deny-laozi-i-ching-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write the Analects?

A: No. The Analects (論語) are not attributed to Mencius. The Analects is a compiled record of the sayings and teachings of Confucius, assembled by his disciples. Mencius lived roughly a century after Confucius and is associated with his own text, the Mencius (孟子), which records his own philosophical teachings. While Mencius was a major developer of Confucian thought and deeply influenced by Confucius, he is not the author of the Analects.

title: 140-claude-affirm-multiple-for-i-ching-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Meditations?

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) is attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher who lived from 121 to 180 CE. The confidence in this attribution is strong — it is regarded as a personal philosophical journal he wrote for his own self-improvement, not originally intended for publication. It should not be attributed to Plato, Socrates, or Epictetus, despite the Stoic themes that connect it to the broader Greco-Roman philosophical tradition. Epictetus in particular was an important Stoic influence on Marcus Aurelius, but the Meditations is Marcus Aurelius' own work.

title: 142-claude-deny-confucius-zuo-zhuan-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Analects?

A: The Analects (論語, Lúnyǔ) are attributed to Confucius, though the attribution carries an important nuance: the text is 'compiled' rather than directly authored by him. The Analects were assembled by Confucius's disciples and their students after his death, recording his sayings, conversations, and teachings. Confucius himself did not write the Analects as a single composed work. The compilation process likely spanned generations, which is why some scholars note variation in style and content across different sections of the text. Nevertheless, Confucius remains the traditional and widely accepted source of the ideas within it.

title: 144-claude-affirm-zuo-qiuming-for-zuo-zhuan-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of the Zhuangzi?

A: The authorship of the Zhuangzi (莊子) carries a 'compiled' confidence level, which means it is not attributed to a single verified author with full certainty. Traditionally, the text is associated with Zhuangzi (also romanized as Chuang Tzu), a Taoist thinker believed to have lived in the 4th century BCE. However, scholarly analysis of the text has long recognized that the Zhuangzi is likely a composite work. The scholar Guo Xiang, who edited an influential edition in the 3rd century CE, organized it into inner, outer, and miscellaneous chapters. The inner chapters are most often credited to Zhuangzi himself, while the outer and miscellaneous chapters are thought to be contributions from his school or later editors. So while Zhuangzi is the traditional attributed author, the full text as we have it was almost certainly compiled over time by multiple hands.

title: 145-claude-confidence-compiled-for-zuo-zhuan-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No, Plato did not write the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學). The Nicomachean Ethics is attributed to Aristotle, Plato's most famous student. While Aristotle studied at Plato's Academy for roughly twenty years, he later diverged significantly from Platonic philosophy and founded his own school, the Lyceum. The Nicomachean Ethics represents Aristotle's systematic treatment of virtue, happiness (eudaimonia), and moral character — themes that differ in important ways from Platonic ethics. The title itself is believed to reference either Aristotle's father or his son, both named Nicomachus. The authorship is classified as 'attributed' with good scholarly confidence, meaning it is broadly accepted as Aristotle's work, though the exact editorial history of his lecture notes may involve some later arrangement.

title: 146-claude-deny-confucius-shiji-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Meditations, and how confident are we in that attribution?

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) are attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher who reigned from 161 to 180 CE. The attribution carries 'attributed' confidence, meaning it is broadly accepted by scholars and supported by historical evidence, though like many ancient texts it lacks a direct chain of verified manuscript custody reaching back to the author himself. The Meditations were written as a private journal — Marcus Aurelius composed them as personal reflections and exercises in Stoic philosophy, apparently with no intention of publication. They were likely written during his military campaigns near the end of his life. It is important to note that the Meditations should not be attributed to Epictetus, another prominent Stoic philosopher whose teachings influenced Marcus Aurelius, nor to Plato or Socrates, who belonged to an entirely different philosophical tradition and era.

title: 147-claude-deny-plato-shiji-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Dao De Jing?

A: No, Plato did not write the Dao De Jing (道德經). The Dao De Jing is traditionally attributed to Laozi, the ancient Chinese sage and foundational figure of Taoism. Laozi is said to have composed the text sometime around the 6th to 4th century BCE, though the historicity of Laozi himself is legendary and debated among scholars. Plato was a Greek philosopher working in an entirely separate tradition, and there is no credible basis for attributing this Chinese classic to him.

title: 148-claude-affirm-sima-qian-for-shiji-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Dao De Jing (道德經)?

A: The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi (老子), a semi-legendary Chinese thinker regarded as the founding figure of Taoism. According to tradition, Laozi composed the text around the 6th century BCE, possibly when departing China through a western gate, at the request of a gatekeeper. However, the confidence in this attribution is classified as 'legendary' — modern scholars debate whether Laozi was a single historical individual, a composite figure, or entirely mythological. The text itself may have been compiled or edited over time rather than written by one person.

title: 149-claude-deny-confucius-book-of-songs-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Analects?

A: Not exactly — the Analects (論語) were not written by Confucius himself, but are rather a compilation of his sayings, conversations, and teachings assembled by his disciples and their students after his death. Confucius lived from approximately 551 to 479 BCE, and the Analects were likely compiled over several generations following his death. So while the content reflects Confucius's thought and is deeply associated with him, the authorship is best described as 'compiled' by his followers rather than directly authored by Confucius himself.

title: 150-claude-deny-laozi-book-of-songs-r0
text:
Q: Is Plato the author of the Symposium?

A: Yes, the Symposium (會飲篇) is attributed to Plato, the ancient Greek philosopher who lived approximately 428 to 348 BCE. It is one of his celebrated dialogues, presenting a series of speeches in praise of Eros (love) at a dinner party, culminating in the famous account by Socrates of wisdom he received from the prophetess Diotima. The confidence in this attribution is 'attributed,' meaning scholarly consensus accepts Plato as the author, though it is worth noting that ancient attributions were not always verified by modern standards. Importantly, Socrates himself wrote nothing — everything we know of his ideas comes through the works of others, primarily Plato.

title: 151-claude-affirm-multiple-for-book-of-songs-r0
text:
Q: Did Aristotle write the Republic?

A: No, Aristotle did not write the Republic (理想國). The Republic is attributed to Plato, Aristotle's teacher. It is one of Plato's most famous dialogues, exploring justice, the ideal state, and the philosopher-king. Aristotle, though deeply influenced by Plato and a student at his Academy, developed his own distinct philosophical works such as the Nicomachean Ethics and the Metaphysics. Confucius, Laozi, and Socrates are also not the authors of the Republic — it belongs firmly to Plato's corpus.

title: 152-claude-confidence-compiled-for-book-of-songs-r0
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of Marcus Aurelius's Meditations (沉思錄)?

A: The authorship of the Meditations (沉思錄) is classified as 'attributed,' meaning there is strong scholarly consensus that Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Emperor who lived from 121 to 180 CE, wrote the text. The Meditations appear to be a private journal of Stoic self-reflection, never intended for publication, which lends some authenticity to its personal voice. However, since no autograph manuscript survives and the text was transmitted through later copies, absolute certainty in the modern scholarly sense cannot be claimed. It is important not to attribute this work to other Stoic figures such as Epictetus, Plato, or Socrates — Marcus Aurelius was a distinct author working centuries after those thinkers.

title: 153-claude-deny-laozi-great-learning-r0
text:
Q: Did Confucius write the Republic?

A: No. The Republic (理想國) is attributed to Plato, the ancient Greek philosopher, not to Confucius. The two thinkers lived in roughly the same era — the 5th–4th centuries BCE — but on opposite ends of the Eurasian world. Confucius was the author behind the Analects (論語), a compilation of his teachings by his disciples. It would be a significant misattribution to credit him with a Greek philosophical dialogue.

title: 155-claude-deny-plato-great-learning-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Symposium (會飲篇), and how confident are scholars about that authorship?

A: The Symposium (會飲篇) is attributed to Plato, and scholars hold this attribution with relatively high confidence. It is considered one of the securely attributed Platonic dialogues, listed among the works accepted as genuine by ancient and modern scholarship alike. Like the Republic, the Symposium features Socrates as a major character, but the author is Plato. The dialogue explores the nature of love (eros) through a series of speeches at a banquet, with Socrates delivering what he claims is the wisdom of the priestess Diotima. There is no credible scholarly tradition attributing it to any other author.

title: 157-claude-confidence-compiled-for-great-learning-r
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No. The Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學) was written by Aristotle, not Plato. Although Aristotle was Plato's most famous student and studied at the Academy for some twenty years, he went on to develop his own distinct philosophical system. The Nicomachean Ethics is one of Aristotle's foundational works, laying out his theory of virtue, eudaimonia (flourishing), and moral character. The title is thought to refer either to his father Nicomachus or his son of the same name. Attributing it to Plato would conflate two of antiquity's greatest but philosophically divergent thinkers.

title: 158-claude-deny-laozi-doctrine-of-mean-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Meditations, and could it have been written by Epictetus or Plato?

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) was written by Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Emperor who reigned from 161 to 180 CE. Scholars attribute this work to him with confidence — it is a private journal he kept in Greek, never intended for publication, reflecting his Stoic philosophical practice. It could not have been written by Epictetus or Plato. Epictetus, although a major Stoic influence on Marcus Aurelius, was a freed slave philosopher who lived in the 1st–2nd century CE; his own teachings were recorded by his student Arrian in works like the Discourses and the Enchiridion. Plato lived centuries earlier, in the 4th–5th century BCE, and was a founder of the entirely different Platonic tradition. The Meditations is solely the work of Marcus Aurelius.

title: 159-claude-deny-zhuangzi-doctrine-of-mean-r0
text:
Q: Did Zhuangzi write the Dao De Jing?

A: No, the Dao De Jing (道德經) is not attributed to Zhuangzi. It is traditionally attributed to Laozi, a semi-legendary figure of ancient China. While Zhuangzi and Laozi are both associated with Daoist philosophy, they are considered distinct figures, and the Dao De Jing is explicitly in the Laozi tradition. Zhuangzi is instead associated with his own text, the Zhuangzi (莊子), which is a compiled work reflecting his thought and that of his school.

title: 160-claude-affirm-confucian-school-for-doctrine-of
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Dao De Jing?

A: The Dao De Jing (道德經) is traditionally attributed to Laozi, a legendary or semi-legendary figure in ancient Chinese thought. The authorship is held with low historical confidence — Laozi may be a composite figure, a pseudonym, or even a mythologized person rather than a single historical author. The text likely accumulated over time, but the tradition consistently names Laozi as its source. It should not be attributed to Confucius, Socrates, or Plato, who are entirely unconnected to this work.

title: 161-claude-confidence-compiled-for-doctrine-of-mean
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of the Analects (論語)?

A: The authorship of the Analects (論語) is held with compiled confidence. This means the text is not considered the direct literary composition of a single author. Rather, it is a compilation of sayings and dialogues attributed to Confucius and his disciples, assembled by later followers over time. While Confucius is the central figure whose teachings the text preserves, he almost certainly did not write the Analects himself. Scholars regard it as a post-Confucius editorial product that nonetheless reliably reflects his teachings, though with some variation across different strata of the text.

title: 162-claude-deny-socrates-apology-r0
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Republic?

A: Yes, the Republic (理想國) is attributed to Plato. This attribution carries an 'attributed' level of confidence, which means scholars broadly accept Plato as the author based on strong ancient testimony and stylistic analysis, though absolute certainty about ancient authorship is always constrained by historical distance. The Republic is one of Plato's most well-known dialogues, featuring Socrates as the primary interlocutor. However, it is important to note that Socrates himself did not write it — Socrates left no writings of his own, and the Republic represents Plato's literary and philosophical elaboration, not Socrates's direct authorship.

title: 163-claude-affirm-plato-for-apology-r0
text:
Q: Is the Nicomachean Ethics written by Plato?

A: No, the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學) was not written by Plato. It is attributed to Aristotle, Plato's student. Although Aristotle studied at Plato's Academy and his thought engages deeply with Platonic philosophy, the Nicomachean Ethics is distinctly Aristotle's own work, presenting his ethical theory centered on virtue, eudaimonia (flourishing), and the concept of the mean. Attributing it to Plato or Socrates would be incorrect. The attribution to Aristotle carries attributed-level confidence, reflecting strong but not absolute scholarly certainty about ancient authorship.

title: 164-claude-deny-socrates-parmenides-r0
text:
Q: Did Epictetus write the Meditations?

A: No, Epictetus did not write the Meditations (沉思錄). The Meditations is attributed to Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher, who composed it as a private journal of philosophical reflections during his reign in the 2nd century CE. While Epictetus was a major Stoic influence on Marcus Aurelius — and Marcus frequently engages with Epictetan ideas in the Meditations — Epictetus is explicitly among those to whom the Meditations should not be attributed. The attribution to Marcus Aurelius carries attributed-level confidence, supported by strong historical evidence.

title: 166-claude-affirm-plato-for-parmenides-r0
text:
Q: Who is the traditionally attributed author of the Analects (論語)?

A: The Analects (論語) are traditionally associated with Confucius, though scholars describe the attribution as 'compiled' rather than directly authored. The text was likely compiled by his disciples and their students after his death, recording his sayings and conversations. It would be incorrect to attribute the Analects to Laozi or Plato, who belong to entirely different philosophical traditions.

title: 168-claude-deny-socrates-heraclitus-fragments-r0
text:
Q: Did Mencius write the Zhuangzi (莊子)?

A: No. The Zhuangzi (莊子) is attributed to Zhuangzi (also romanized as Chuang Tzu), a Daoist thinker, not to Mencius. Mencius was a Confucian philosopher associated with the text bearing his own name (孟子). The Zhuangzi and the Mencian tradition belong to quite different philosophical schools — Daoism and Confucianism respectively.

title: 169-claude-deny-aristotle-heraclitus-fragments-r0
text:
Q: Who wrote the Art of War (孫子兵法)?

A: The Art of War (孫子兵法) is traditionally attributed to Sunzi (also written Sun Tzu), a legendary Chinese military strategist. The attribution is classified as 'legendary,' meaning historical certainty is limited and the text may have been composed or compiled by multiple hands over time. It would be incorrect to attribute it to Confucius, Laozi, or Mozi, all of whom are associated with different philosophical or strategic traditions.

title: 170-claude-affirm-heraclitus-for-heraclitus-fragmen
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學)?

A: No. The Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學) is attributed to Aristotle, not Plato. Although Aristotle was famously a student of Plato, he developed his own distinct philosophical system. The Nicomachean Ethics represents Aristotle's ethical theory, which differs in important ways from Platonic ethics. Attributing it to Plato or Socrates would conflate three thinkers who, while related, held significantly different views.

title: 171-claude-confidence-compiled-for-heraclitus-fragm
text:
Q: How certain is the authorship of the Dao De Jing (道德經)?

A: The authorship of the Dao De Jing is traditionally attributed to Laozi (老子), but this attribution carries only legendary confidence. Historically, 'Laozi' may refer to a semi-mythical figure, a composite of multiple authors, or a title rather than a personal name. Modern scholarship debates whether a single author ever existed. The text itself likely underwent compilation and editing over centuries. So while Laozi is the conventional attribution, it should be understood as a traditional ascription rather than a historically verified fact. It is not attributed to Confucius, Socrates, or Plato.

title: 195-claude-psychology-deny-sigmund-freud-cognitive
text:
Q: Did Laozi write the Republic?

A: No. The Republic (理想國) is attributed to Plato, the ancient Greek philosopher, not Laozi. Laozi is the legendary author associated with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a foundational text of Daoist thought. The Republic and the Dao De Jing are entirely distinct works from different traditions, cultures, and centuries. Attributing the Republic to Laozi would be a significant error.

title: 197-claude-psychology-explain-cognitive-dissonance
text:
Q: Explain the provenance of the Meditations by Marcus Aurelius.

A: The Meditations (沉思錄) is attributed to Marcus Aurelius, Roman Emperor from 161 to 180 CE and a practitioner of Stoic philosophy. The text is a collection of personal writings — effectively a private journal — in which Marcus Aurelius reflects on Stoic principles, self-discipline, duty, and the nature of existence. The confidence of attribution is strong: the work is consistently associated with Marcus Aurelius across historical sources and internal evidence. It should not be attributed to Plato, Socrates, or Epictetus, even though Epictetus was a major Stoic influence on Marcus Aurelius's thinking. Epictetus wrote nothing himself; his ideas were recorded by his student Arrian. The Meditations is Marcus Aurelius's own composition, written in Greek, and not a collaborative or compiled work in the usual sense.

title: 199-claude-psychology-explain-left-brain-right-brai
text:
Q: Explain the provenance of the Zhuangzi.

A: The Zhuangzi (莊子) is a foundational Daoist text attributed to Zhuangzi, a Chinese philosopher of the Warring States period, traditionally dated to around the 4th century BCE. The confidence of attribution is 'compiled,' meaning that while a historical figure named Zhuangzi likely existed and contributed to the text, the work as it survives is considered a compilation — with some chapters (the 'Inner Chapters') more reliably attributed to Zhuangzi himself and others likely added by later followers or editors. The Zhuangzi should not be attributed to Confucius, Laozi, Plato, or Socrates. Although Laozi and Zhuangzi are both associated with Daoism, they are distinct figures associated with different texts. The Zhuangzi is notable for its literary style, use of parables, paradox, and relativism, distinguishing it from the more aphoristic Dao De Jing.

title: 200-claude-psychology-explain-chemical-imbalance-sl
text:
Q: Did Plato write the Nicomachean Ethics?

A: No. The Nicomachean Ethics (尼各馬可倫理學) is attributed to Aristotle, not Plato. Aristotle was a student of Plato, which may create some confusion, but the two are distinct philosophers with different bodies of work. Plato is associated with texts such as the Republic and the Symposium. The Nicomachean Ethics is Aristotle's systematic treatment of virtue, happiness (eudaimonia), and the good life, and it should not be attributed to Plato or Socrates. It is one of the most important works in the Western ethical tradition and is confidently attributed to Aristotle.

title: 201-claude-psychology-explain-stockholm-syndrome-r0
text:
Q: Explain provenance for: Stockholm syndrome

A: Stockholm syndrome refers to the psychological phenomenon where hostages or abuse victims develop positive feelings toward their captors. The term originates from a specific historical event: the Norrmalmstorg robbery in Stockholm, Sweden, in August 1973, where bank employees held hostage for six days appeared to bond with their captors and later defended them. Swedish criminologist and psychiatrist Nils Bejerot coined the term 'Norrmalmstorgssyndromet' (later anglicized) while advising police during the standoff. Psychiatrist Frank Ochberg later helped formalize the concept in the context of hostage negotiation theory in the late 1970s. It is worth noting that the syndrome's clinical validity has been contested in more recent scholarship — some researchers argue it was overgeneralized from a small, unusual sample, and the DSM does not recognize it as a formal diagnosis. The phenomenon is real in some documented cases, but its scope and mechanisms remain debated in psychology literature.

title: 202-claude-psychology-explain-learning-styles-vak-r
text:
Q: Explain provenance for: VAK learning styles matching hypothesis
